text: So this is a robotic left upper lobe lobectomy in a 75-year-old man.
He had a fairly central 3 centimeter tumor.
He was treated with induction immunotherapy when it came for surgical resection.
And he had a fairly incomplete fissure.
And so what we're attempting to do, at least, is to perform a left upper lobectomy without having to dissect the fissure.
So this is a fissureless approach.
The ports are all placed in a single line over the 8th interspace, so over the top of the 9th rib.
We have a 12mm port in the anterior and most inferior aspect, a camera port sort of in the mid-axillary line, slightly anterior to it,
and then two additional 8mm ports between the spine and the camera port, sort of spread equidistantly.
In this case I'm using a 5mm port between the camera port and the 12mm port, about a rib space below.
This is an air seal port, we're using air seal for CO2 insufflation.
In this patient, we have performed a posterior intercostal block already.
We do that at the beginning of the case under thoracoscopic vision.
And so I start out performing a fairly detailed mediastinal lymph node dissection.
I find that by performing the nodal dissection at the beginning of the case that I do a more thorough resection of the lymph nodes.
I also think that it really helps clear the lymph node tissue away from the vessels and the bronchi and really sort of sets the case up for division of the bronchovascular structures later on.
So here we're just taking down the inferior pulmonary ligament.
And I would say the important thing here, there's two things.
First of all, you want to get all the nodal tissue out, and you want to dissect on that plane in between the ligament and the lung.
But you've got to be careful not to

buzz the lung not to cause air leaks in the lung and it is pretty easy sometimes you'll find that the lung tissue extends down more on one side than the other on the ligament so it's a good idea in general to flip the lung backwards and forth to make sure that you are not entering lung tissue

and then the other thing is is that these nodes receive their vascular supply generally from the um medial and and deep aspect and if you don't buzz those vessels well sometimes they'll retract on you and that you'll think they'll have stopped but they can be a source of post-operative hemorrhage so i really make a point to to really make sure that those vessels are very well cauterized
So in this case, the nodal specimen is being taken out by withdrawing the instrument in the anterior 12mm port and then placing an assistant 5mm grasper through that.
So once that's done, then the retraction is adjusted a little bit more.
We're going to march up posteriorly dividing the pleura behind the hilum, the posterior aspect of the hilum.
And my intention here is to identify the inferior pulmonary ligament and the left main stem bronchus.
And then we're going to clear out the nodes that are
situated between the bronchus and the inferior pulmonary ligament as well as those between the pulmonary artery and the bronchus.
So I want to get right down onto the pulmonary vein.
A good adage that I remember being told to me is dissect on named structures.
So be that the bronchus, pulmonary artery, the pulmonary vein, in general if you know where the important structures are then you're less likely to get into them.
So you generally have to elevate the lung parenchyma off of the
bronchus and the vessels.
And here we're just taking very small little bites.
There's that station 10.
And that's sitting cephalad to the bronchus there.
So again, constantly readjusting the retraction.
So in this situation, because we freed up the inferior pulmonary ligament, his lower lobe is wanting to get into our way here, so we'll just use the assistant to retract that lung for us.
The other thing you can do is leave a little bit of the inferior pulmonary ligament attached at its caudal aspect, dissecting everything else out.
That sort of tethers that lower lobe down to the diaphragm and prevents it getting up in your face like it has been doing.
So I try to dissect around the node and try to dissect the node away from the surrounding tissues, but I try not to grab the node itself at this point.
So we're just applying a little bit of energy right at the edge of the node.
And then we'll use a little bit of spreading and blunt retraction to further elevate that node and divide the perinodal tissue.

and I generally don't pick up the node until I've mobilized it about 60 to 70% because what I find is that if I take a little bite of the node I'm much more likely to fracture the node and then it just bleeds and it's a nuisance.
So blunt retraction ideal and then really mobilize the node before you take a
larger bite of it.
I use these 5cm cigars and gauze rolls.
I just find that the 10cm ones tend to be a little bit bulky and they get in the way.
These work pretty well.
So we're attracting the lung using a gauze roll that's held with a grasper.
so that that provides a little bit more traction.
I try not to pick up on the lung directly with my retraction.
Retraction is currently in the posterior most important arm four.
and it has to be constantly readjusted.
As you release the pleura and the pilar tissue to get the same amount of traction, you've got to push on that lung just a little bit more, so there's sort of this constant readjustment.
So now this is a station 10L that's situated between the left main bronchus and the inferior pulmonary vein.

And we're going to take this out completely, and then that's going to set us up for doing our station 7, our mesocrinal lymph node dissection, which is, I find, a little harder on the left side because you tend to be working very much in a hole.
It's tucked all the way up and under that...
that left main bronchus.
The left main bronchus being significantly longer as well than the right main stem bronchus.
So you have a little bit more work to do to get the subcranial nodes out on the left side.
So you're helping get exposure by using the assistant there to push down on the inferior pulmonary vein.
I often don't use an assist port, particularly for right-sided resections, but I think for left-sided resections it can be very helpful.
So now we're going to do the subcarinal lymph node dissection and you can see that the assistant is providing retraction anteriorly by pushing down on the pericardium there.
I think one of the things you have to be very, very careful of here is not tenting up that main stem bronchus too much because you have a fairly rigid
double lumen endotracheal tube in there usually, and certainly that can push its way through the back wall of that bronchus, through the membranous portion of the bronchus.
So there you can see the esophagus and one of the subcranial nodes.
I'm going to try to separate the node away from the esophagus.
So I think you can see the contralateral airway right at the base in there.
Right there.
So I'll resect as much nodal tissue as I possibly can from here, although I think I don't get quite as complete a node dissection as I do on the right side, just because of the depth.
And I certainly don't want to get bleeding down there, if at all possible.
We do get a good sampling of the nodal tissue, though.
So now we're just going to keep on marching up cephalad towards the head and again releasing the pleura and taking out any station 10 lymph nodes.
So at this point I'll try to identify the vagus and just sort of travel up along the left vagus.

You've got to be careful in this region here because there are several bronchial vessels that you will encounter and just need to make sure that you buzz those well, which I didn't do in this situation.
An additional level 10.
Then we're going to start mobilizing the lung parenchyma and nodal tissue off of the posterior main pulmonary artery there.
This is going to really help us when we come to divide the fissure later on.
But I do it now just because the exposure is pretty good.
So we're starting to see the interlobar pulmonary artery there, branch to the upper lobe above it.
And that looks like the posterior trunk up there.
So that looks like a vagus branch that's going directly to the lung.
So we can probably divide that one.

and staying right on top of the vagus there dividing tissue that is superficial to it generally that is safe gotta be a little bit careful as you obviously approach the ligament to arteriosum that you're not getting too near the recurrent but generally staying anterior to the vagus works well
And once we have this dissected out, now we're going to flip the lung.
We've done about as much posterior dissection as I can.
I'm going to flip the lung posteriorly now, and then attack this from the front.
As you can see, we've already reached the aortic pulmonary window area here.

So we're going to do the same thing dividing the pleura, this time on the anterior aspect of the thalam and identify where our superior pulmonary vein is and where the pulmonary artery and truncus is.
So that looks like PA.
And there's our AI over pulmonary window node.
That we actually dissected partially from the other side.
Again, because we've released the pleura, we can then readjust our retraction a little bit more, just to get a little bit more tension on the tissues.
Follow that vagus nerve all the way up.
The blunt dissection can be helpful here.
you get a sense where the recurrent laryngeal nerve is coming off there.
So at this point I'll generally try to divide the pleura anterior to where I'm going to be dissecting between the phrenic nerve and the aortic pulmonary window node.
This sort of defines my anterior most boundary, just makes it easier for me at least when I'm dissecting out that node to know where to stop.
That's because I obviously don't want to dissect too far anteriorly for a risk of injury to the phrenic.
And then in this area, I'm just sort of staying right on top of that, right beside that lymph node, dissecting right on it.
taking care to stay away from the current.
Again, checking where my anterior boundary of dissection is there, where the phrenic nerve is staying away from it.
Now we'll just do a little bit more.

dissection anterior to the hilum there's sometimes some station 10 nodes in here so we'll take those out we've got to release this anyhow because we've got to get around the superior pony veins so this is dissection that needs to be done in any case but I'll just do it now because of the exposure there's our area of previous dissection around the
This fatty tissue can sometimes be quite vascular, so again, making sure that it's well cauterized before you pull the tissue apart is helpful.
Saves time going back to fixed bleeding.
maybe fat, maybe nodes, but we'll send it off anyhow.
So just like we did before, posteriorly, now we're going to dissect the lung tissue off of the bronchus and the superior veins.
This is again going to aid us in
performing the fissural dissection later on in the case.
So we're going to be removing that interlobar node now.
I think it's always important to have a free peanut or a sponge in the chest just in case you encounter bleeding.
It's useful to have just so you can grab it, push it on the vessel that's bleeding and provide some instant tamponade.
So we're about to put a vessel loop around the superior pulmonary vein, and then we'll divide that.
In this case, this vessel loop was a little bit too short.
The longer the vessel loop is, the easier it is to get the two ends together.
So about a centimeter or two longer would have been nice in this case.
Now, usually I will use the Sureform 45 curve tip.
And in this particular case, for whatever reason, a flat tip was opened.
And so it's not ideal, but it can still do the job.
But that curved tip stapler is really, really helpful, I find, getting around vessels safely.
So I know a lot of people divide these structures by putting in a posterior port.
And you can see how that right hand instrument, that curved bipolar dissector, is straight in line with that vessel.
So if you have a 12 millimeter port,
between your camera port and your most posterior port.
It certainly makes it a whole lot easier to divide the vein and sometimes some of the bronchus as well and the artery.
But with the Sureform, I rarely ever need to do that.
The angulation of the Sureform stapler is better than the previous versions of the stapler.
And so you can see that all of this can be accomplished by placing the stapler through the anterior port.
You just got to get the angles right, bend your wrist, move things a little bit.
I won't say I never put a posterior port in, a posterior 12 port, but I can count the fingers of one hand the number of times I've had to do it, even using the older staplers.
So now we're going to spend a little time digging out the station 12 nodes, the lobar nodes.
And this is important so that we can dissect out the bronchus and the pulmonary arteries.
Getting the nodes out of the way is the key to this whole procedure.
There's usually fairly good blood supply to these nodes, and it comes off from those bronchial arteries that travel along the bronchus.
So here we're encountering some of the area where we dissected from posteriorly.
And because we've done that posterior dissection, this is a lot easier.
you

When you're getting these nodes out that are sort of wedged in between the vessels, you've got to usually pull them in multiple directions, you know, and sort of, you know, dissect whatever little strands you can see that are under tension.
But you've got to look at it from multiple ways, because you'll free up one, then you pull it a little bit more, you get a little bit more traction on it, another one will come into view.
Just work your way around the node.
I'm using a zero degree scope for this.
I generally don't use the 30 degree unless I really can't see up over the top of the lung, which of course I'll put it in.
I think the 30 degree scope, although sometimes useful, puts a lot of traction on the rib spaces and so I tend to avoid using it.
So in this patient it looks like he has a main truncus posterior as well as a secondary posterior segmental branch and a fairly incomplete fissure as you can see.
So you can just see there that we reached the other side where we were digging out that node.
So I'm reasonably confident that all of the arteries are fully dissected at this point.
But you've got to be really careful because sometimes there can be an additional artery that comes off the back of that thing, that truncus.
The other thing here is that bronchus is preventing us from
being able to divide both of those vessels at the same time.
So by dividing this superior one, our angle can be changed a little bit so that it's more posterior when we pass the stapler in to divide that inferior trunk.
So now that vessel out of the way, now the angle again is going to be more favorable.
Despite having had immunotherapy, the dissection on this patient was actually quite easy.
There wasn't a lot of fibrosis or inflammation that you often find following immunotherapy.
Now we'll address that posterior segmental artery.
Sort of testing the line fire there.
Again, I can only do that because I've dissected all of that nodal tissue out from behind where that artery was.
If I didn't do that, there wouldn't be a big enough window for me to effortlessly pass the stapler.
So, can't stress enough the importance of getting the nodes out.
So now all we should have left is the bronchus to divide.
So we're going to just try to separate the bronchus away from the interlobar pulmonary artery there.
So now we'll remove additional interlobar lymph nodes between the left upper and left lower lobe bronchus.
At this point we'll try to get around the bronchus.
So I'm carefully picking up on the bronchus.
Now you've got to be careful with this because certainly in patients who have calcific bronchi it is possible by grabbing the bronchus to tear it or perforate it.
Certainly a posterior 12mm port would be pretty handy here.
But because it's a bigger port and it does probably put a little bit more pressure on the intercostal nerve, I tend not to like to put those 12mm where the rib spaces are narrower.
We'll be alright with sure form through the entry report here anyhow.
Just getting hung up on a little bit of tissue at the back, but I think we're across.
So now we have our airway divided, got the vessels divided, and it should be a simple matter now of just completing the fissure.

But before you do, you've got to make sure that you get all of those nodes out of there, because you really don't want to be incorporating any nodes in your staple line, ideally, especially if there's a possibility of there being a residual tumor there.
So having done that, it's time to get the fissure divided.
And typically you'll use blue loads, sometimes green, depending on the thickness of the fissure.
Just making sure that the stapler is above the interlobar pulmonary artery, that we're getting all of the upper lobe tissue.
So there you have it.
Fissureless dissection.
And we'll enlarge the anterior 12mm port site now.
And put a specimen retrieval bag into the chest.
We use this thing called an anchor bag.
It's ripstop nylon.
It's virtually indestructible.
Generally just make about a 3-4cm incision.
We'll also do a timeout prior to closure just to make sure that we have all those little vessel loops and sponges and peanuts and so on out of the chest that they're all accounted for.
I'll pass the chest tube reverse into the chest through the small thoracotomy incision and then pull it out through that assist port.
Bronco stump is checked.
Airtight.
And the instruments were thrown.
